Why are you interested in this role?: Program operations isn't just about keeping a tracker updated—from my experience, it's a continuous cycle of onboarding experts cleanly, making sure they can actually access what they need, and turning the operational signal into better decisions through cross-functional collaboration. That's close to what I already do. At InGenius Prep I work alongside subject-matter experts to enhance our programs and to make sure participants have smooth access to the right offerings—running end-to-end workflows and lifecycles for 20+ concurrent programs, partnering across Marketing, Tech, and Sales and 10+ vendors, and keeping records and delivery reliable as we scale. The approach I'd want to bring to the Human Data team's external domain experts is the same one I lean on now: using data analysis and discovery to problem-solve, so the operational foundation actually holds up under real complexity.
Why Anthropic?: Anthropic's focus on empirical science and collaboration resonates deeply with how I approach work—relying on data, internal insights, and stakeholder partnership to design solutions that actually scale. With a master's in Applied Organizational Psychology, I treat organizational challenges as systems to be studied, applying mixed-methods data like interviews, surveys, and feedback loops to pinpoint capability gaps and design solutions that stick. In my current role I recently ran a comprehensive needs assessment with 25+ global stakeholders to uncover gaps in knowledge management—much like an empirical researcher identifies critical variables in a study—and reduced onboarding time by 75% through standardized documentation and a revised communications strategy. For this role, I'd apply that same evidence-based methodology to build the onboarding programs and feedback loops that keep the Human Data team's external domain experts productive and reliable. Ultimately, I want to work at Anthropic because I'm passionate about building human expertise alongside AI technology—and I'm drawn to a company that views its own improvement through the lens of scientific research and data-driven insights.
What excites you about this opportunity?: What excites me is that this role treats operations as load-bearing, not administrative. When the people you're supporting are external experts whose work directly shapes a model, getting their onboarding, access, and data integrity right isn't bureaucracy—it's the foundation everything else rests on. At Vestas I directed an M&A project migrating and merging employee records with zero tolerance for error, and coordinated immigration and relocation across attorneys and internal teams, which sharpened my judgment on prioritization, escalation, and being the reliable point of contact when things get complicated. The chance to build that kind of dependable operational system for the Human Data team, while it's still being shaped, is the part I'd look forward to most.
Tell us about a time you turned operatio: At ODN Oregon, I worked with a small team of consultants supporting an HR client that was carrying a $362K leave-cost problem and assuming it was unavoidable. Rather than hand them an answer, we partnered with their HR team to audit 300+ confidential leave cases, surface what the data actually showed, and refute the cost assumptions with evidence—which led to real policy redesigns and first-aid training. The operational work—the audit, the records, the integrity of the data—was what made the decision credible. It taught me how much it matters to move a conversation from assumptions to evidence, which is exactly how my MS in Applied Organizational Psychology trained me to operate: diagnose with data, collaborate cross-functionally on the fix, then track whether the progress holds.
